next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 10

The scullery off the kitchens smelled of wet stone, old onions, and lamp oil going sharp in the heat. Midnight had come and gone, and Owen still had the Grinlow Leaves spread on an upturned flour bin because it was flatter than the table, and flatter mattered. The page he wanted would hold for perhaps six breaths if his burned right palm pinned the lower margin and the lamp sat exactly level with the nick in the opposite wall. On the seventh breath the ink went pale and the lines slid back under the vellum as if the manuscript had thought better of showing him anything.

He had been stuck there three nights, which was another way of saying nobody downstairs had found a better use for him.

His ash-linen notebook lay open beside it, dense with cramped tries and cross-outs. He had written that the quarter mark repeated at ninety degrees, that the thing was not stars but indexed by stars, not a calendar. He had written the last sentence four times in four different places as if changing where it sat might make it truer.

The kitchens beyond the scullery still muttered at low volume: pan bottoms ringing softly, water thrown into a basin, one of the younger elves laughing and then stopping at once when an older voice shushed him. The ordinary work of the castle went on under the occupation because somebody had to scrub the roasting tins and bank the ovens even when Amycus Carrow was upstairs teaching children to scream properly.

Footsteps came along the service passage, cautious and tired, too heavy for Tallow's quick soft slap.

Prue came in sideways through the...
open_hooks_to_carry: Make Martha's recruitment of Owen emotionally credible: she is still trying to pull him back in.
Build the sabotage with practical wartime details before Owen's amendment derails it.
prev_tail_excerpt:
"I've just been tired."

Martha gave him such a flat look that the lie died where it was.

"Fine," she said. "Be tired, then."

She folded the torn paper into a square so small it disappeared in her fist.

"I keep bringing the wrong things to you," she said. "That's my mistake."

"Martha, that's not-"

"No. Leave it."

She tucked her scarf up under her chin with two hard little jerks, the way she did when both hands wanted a job. Then she stepped around him and started down the corridor toward the barrel door.

Owen moved after her once, then stopped. "Will I see you in the morning?"

She did not turn round.

"You'll see me somewhere," she said.

She gave him only a small lift of the hand, already half on her way to the barrel. At the Hufflepuff door she knocked the right pattern and slipped through when the wood swung aside. The door shut behind her with its usual thick, stupid warmth.

Owen stood in the blue lamp light with the untouched mince pie cooling in his hand.

The corridor smelled of old stone, paraffin, and the spiced fruit Tallow had sent upstairs in paper. Grease bled through the wrapping into his palm until the burn began to throb. One word kept coming back in Martha's voice. Conclude. He looked at the shut barrel door and thought, stupidly, that he could still knock.

He stayed where he was. By the time he made himself turn away, the paper had gone translucent in his fist and the burn in his palm was throbbing under it.
state_deltas_required: The resistance needs a corridor sabotage to work, while Owen is using the same operation as cover for his own route.
Owen's secrecy and divided motive turn him into a liability, and the resistance stops treating him as merely unhelpful.
